summary: Julia Evans reflects on her second full year working independently on Wizard Zines, articulating a mission focused on explaining established technologies and helping people build foundational confidence with computers. She shifted energy toward building educational websites rather than finishing zines, creating several DNS-related tools and playgrounds. She found success collaborating with Marie on Mess With DNS and opened a print zine store. Despite releasing more free content than paid products, the business remains sustainable through zine sales. She also began relaxing her rule against writing anything negative, finding that slightly more critical posts turned out better.
key_points: Her emerging mission is to help people understand the 'simple' core of how computers work so they gain confidence to handle any new complexity thrown at them
She built seven educational websites and tools in 2021, mostly DNS-related, driven by the belief that people learn best through hands-on experimentation rather than reading
She got stuck finishing several zines because she needs a clear mental model of why people struggle with a topic, and her first guesses about that are often wrong
She began intentionally relaxing her 'never write anything negative' blogging rule, finding that slightly more critical posts about DNS propagation and the OSI model were better for it
Collaboration with Marie on Mess With DNS was a highlight, addressing the loneliness of working independently
money_quotes: I don't know what all this nonsense is but at the end of the day it's all just assembly instructions and memory and system calls, I can figure this out
I think the two hardest things about working for myself are working alone and having to decide what to do.
There's a lot of hidden knowledge about these 'old' tools (what are they used for? what are the common mistakes? which parts can you ignore?). I want to make that knowledge easy to find.

We think about debugging as a technical skill (and it absolutely is!!) but a huge amount of it is managing your feelings so you don't get discouraged and being self-aware so you can recognize your incorrect assumptions
The explanation looks something like 'the reason many people struggle with TOPIC is because they don't understand X, here's what you need to know'. My first guess at what X is often wrong.
I want to figure out how to help other people move towards that confidence of 'ok, I know how this computer stuff works, I can figure out any weird new nonsense you throw at me'.
All of the work in this post continues to be 100% funded by zine sales, which I'm grateful for.
key_insight: Understanding the simple foundations of how computers work gives you the confidence to handle any complex new technology, and the best way to teach that is through hands-on experimentation rather than reading.
